incorporations.reason: Italian 'personalità' means personality, not person; Gemini is more literal
Italian 'rintuzzare il nostro slancio' — 'rintuzzare' means to blunt/rebuff and 'slancio' means élan/dash; both words are more precise than Claude's paraphrase

incorporations.original: plants himself squarely across the road to bar their flight
incorporations.replacement: plants himself across the road to bar their way
incorporations.reason: Italian 'per sbarrarla' means to bar it (the road/their way), not to bar their flight; 'squarely' is an addition not in the source

incorporations.original: In that moment of heroism, he had forgotten that motion has its own laws
incorporations.replacement: Heroism forgot that motion has its laws
incorporations.reason: Italian 'L'eroismo dimenticava che il moto ha le sue leggi' — the subject is 'heroism' (abstract personification), not 'he'; Gemini preserves the rhetorical figure

incorporations.original: pinched my cheek with good-natured familiarity
incorporations.reason: Italian 'mi strinse bonariamente la guancia' — 'bonariamente' is a simple adverb; Claude's 'with good-natured familiarity' over-expands it
Italian 'codicillo' is a legal term meaning codicil (an addendum to a will); 'postscript' loses the legalistic flavour Crespi intended
Italian 'che tutta la domina' — 'la' refers to Rome; 'all' is vague, while 'the entire city' makes the antecedent explicit as Gemini does

incorporations.original: a gleam of red — the trousers of a Frenchman
incorporations.replacement: the red trousers of a Frenchman
incorporations.reason: Italian 'vidi rosseggiare... i calzoni di un francese' — the object is the trousers; Claude's 'a gleam of red' adds a dramatic elaboration not in the source

incorporations.original: some members of the Guardia Nazionale
incorporations.replacement: some militiamen of the Guardia Nazionale
incorporations.reason: Italian 'militi della Guardia Nazionale' — 'militi' specifically means militiamen/soldiers, not generic 'members'

incorporations.original: extolling our valour, and giving voice to the wish
incorporations.replacement: extolling our valour and the wish
incorporations.reason: Italian 'esaltare il nostro valore e l'augurio' — the verb 'esaltare' governs both objects; Claude's 'giving voice to' adds a verb not in the source
Italian 'in tale estremo' — 'estremo' as a noun means extremity/dire strait, not 'extreme hour'
Italian 'quale scempio!' — 'scempio' means carnage/havoc/outrage, carrying a sense of desecration that 'massacre' lacks
Italian 'saturando' directly means saturating; 'steeping' is an interpretive substitution
Italian 'cessò di palpitare' — 'palpitare' means to throb/beat (of the heart), not to breathe; the image is of a heart stopping
Italian 'su non altro' means 'if nothing else' — the opposite meaning from 'above all else'; Claude's version inverts the rhetorical point
Italian 'propizie' means propitious; Claude's three-word paraphrase is unnecessarily wordy
Italian 'armi superiori' — 'armi' means arms (the period-appropriate English term); 'weapons' is modern and less literary
Italian 'contingenti freschi' — 'contingenti' means contingents (units of troops), not generic reinforcements
Italian 'un cavaliere sceso' — 'cavaliere' means knight/cavalier; as Masina was a lancer, 'knight' preserves the mounted-warrior connotation that 'warrior' loses

incorporations.original: God in Heaven... what a stench!
incorporations.reason: Italian 'Gesù... che puzza!' — 'Gesù' is Jesus, not 'God in Heaven'; the exclamation should be translated literally
